key_points: Rights are reasonable claims that individuals can make on society and government
In a democracy, rights protect citizens from misuse of power by the government
Fundamental Rights are guaranteed by the Constitution and enforceable by courts
The Right to Equality ensures equality before law and prohibits discrimination
The Right to Freedom provides six freedoms including speech, movement and profession
The Right against Exploitation bans human trafficking, forced labour and child labour
The Right to Freedom of Religion ensures secularism in India
Cultural and Educational Rights protect minority languages, cultures and educational institutions
The Right to Constitutional Remedies allows citizens to approach courts when rights are violated
Courts act as guardians of Fundamental Rights in India
examples: Example of Right to Equality: Equal opportunity in public employment
Example of Freedom of Speech: Citizens criticizing government policies
Example of Right against Exploitation: Laws banning bonded labour
Example of Religious Freedom: Citizens practicing any religion
Example of Cultural Rights: Minority communities preserving their language
Example of Constitutional Remedies: Filing a case in the Supreme Court
formulas: Democratic Rights → Rights that ensure freedom, equality and justice in a democracy
Fundamental Rights → Basic rights guaranteed by the Constitution
PIL (Public Interest Litigation) → Legal action taken for public interest
RTI (Right to Information) → Law allowing citizens to obtain information from government
exam_tips: Remember that Fundamental Rights are enforceable by courts
Understand the difference between Rights and Human Rights
Courts can issue writs to enforce Fundamental Rights
Minority rights protect cultural identity in a diverse country like India
RTI improves transparency and accountability in governance
